headline: Bestinet pocketed RM400M running Malaysia's visa system — with no contract, no oversight, and an Interpol arrest request
context: Bloomberg's 10-month investigation found Bestinet founder Aminul Islam's companies generated over RM400 million in profit from Malaysia's Foreign Workers Centralised Management System. Workers pay up to RM25,000 in recruitment costs. MACC closed its probe in September 2024. Cabinet extended the contract through 2031.

cards.big: One company runs Malaysia's migrant visa system. Its founder faces an Interpol-channel arrest request. The government just extended his contract through 2031.
cards.sub: Bloomberg found Bestinet's founder generated RM400M in profit. Workers pay RM25,000 each. MACC closed its probe in September 2024. Cabinet approved the extension the month before.

cards.big: Six years with no signed contract. Then the per-worker fee was set at RM215 — more than double the government's own approved rate of RM86.
cards.sub: PAC found 24 unauthorized users approved employer applications. The government had no access to the source code. The contract now requires handover before 2031 expiry.

cards.big: Bangladesh requested arrest through Interpol channels. Malaysia asked Bangladesh to drop its trafficking probes to protect its own TIP ranking.
cards.sub: 29% of migrant domestic workers — predominantly women excluded from Employment Act protections — met ILO forced-labour indicators. Employers must legally pay permit fees but workers bear the cost.

cards.big: Zahid created the 10-agency restriction. Anwar extended the contract despite his own research centre's warning. A 2023 open tender attracted only two bidders.
cards.sub: The system processes visas in 14 days versus 120 before. Every coalition has left it in place because dismantling it is costly, risky, and would disrupt labour supply.

cards.big: Malaysian businesses across all communities profit from bonded cheap labour while working-class Malaysians bear the wage suppression
cards.sub: US$1 billion siphoned from Bangladesh. Sabah and Sarawak have a separate, equally severe crisis with 1 million undocumented migrants outside this system entirely.

cards.big: Like outsourcing passport control to a toll booth operator who cut wait times — then discovering the operator charges five times the rate, has no contract, and faces an arrest request
cards.sub: Speed built on worker debt bondage is not efficiency. It is exploitation with a dashboard.

cards.big: The system persists because Malaysia's political and business elites across every community profit from depressed labour costs. The fix exists — open tendering, employer-pays enforcement, cooperation with Bangladesh. No coalition will accept the disruption.
